item.title: Trump and the New American Era of Petrolunacy
item.description: The idea that the U.S. is immune to the oil crisis is a mistaken one.

item.title: How We Chose What to Ask and When to Challenge Tucker Carlson
item.description: “The Interview” host Lulu Garcia-Navarro talks about her preparation to interview Mr. Carlson and some judgment calls she had to make in real time.

item.title: Rich People Didn’t Look Like This Before
item.description: Cosmetic surgery is a sign of making it in the most Kardashian-coded way — get rich, then buy a face.

item.title: Trump Is the One Without the Cards at the Poker Table
item.description: A.I. will drastically increase the power of small states and groups in conflict with the great powers.

item.title: The Supreme Court Steps Backward on Voting and Race
item.description: Readers discuss what one calls “the most recent Supreme Court assault on the Voting Rights Act of 1965.” Also: Shifts in drug policy.

item.title: What’s Lost When We Give Up Driving
item.description: Will self-driving cars mark the end of a rite of passage for teens? On “Interesting Times,” the columnist Ross Douthat asks the author Andrew Miller whether the decline of manual driving signifies a broader shift toward the “safety focus and screenification of American youth.”

item.title: Building a World ‘Quite Unlike Our Own’
item.description: How can the left deliver on its promises if it can’t even navigate its own “horrendous” bureaucracy? On “The Ezra Klein Show,” the Opinion columnist Ezra Klein reflects on Senator Bernie Sanders’s frustrations with government, and argues that government efficiency is the essential “common sense” foundation for a radical vision of American abundance.

item.title: Don’t Underestimate the Fury of Democratic Voters
item.description: A Democratic vision of the Tea Party is emerging.

item.title: He Signed Away His Right to Sue by Subscribing to Disney+
item.description: Thanks to a series of Supreme Court decisions, nearly 80 percent of Fortune 500 companies can — and do — use forced arbitration with consumers or workers.

item.title: The Loneliness of Donald Trump
item.description: This is a president who rarely travels beyond the confines of the White House compound or Mar-a-Lago.

item.title: India Is Prototyping a Dangerous New Model of Web Censorship
item.description: The Modi administration’s systematic throttling of free speech sets a dangerous precedent for the world.

item.title: This Is What’s Behind Trump’s Relentlessness
item.description: “Animal spirits” and American presidents.

item.title: Profound Autism Is Difficult Enough Without This Debunked Method
item.description: Study after study has called facilitated communication into question.

item.title: There’s a 900-Year-Old Answer to Our Most Modern Problem
item.description: What should we do when a chatbot behaves like a criminal?

item.title: What if Your Waymo or Tesla Hallucinates?
item.description: Self-driving cars can still make mistakes, sometimes with frightening consequences. On “Interesting Times,” the author Andrew Miller joins the columnist Ross Douthat to discuss the inhuman errors these cars sometimes make, and whether they can be hacked.

item.title: The U.S. Military Was Losing Its Edge. After Iran, Everyone Knows It.
item.description: Somehow, the weaker nation is in the stronger negotiating position.

item.title: The Tragic Decline of the American Navy
item.description: A 19th-century military strategist should be required reading at the Pentagon.

item.title: What Body Cam Footage Reveals About ICE’s Tactics
item.description: New body camera footage from Nashville reveals a disturbing pattern of ICE and state troopers using minor traffic stops to target Black and brown drivers.

item.title: In a Small Iowa Town, a Solution to a National Crisis
item.description: As the Trump administration guts weather agencies, a state’s low-cost flood tracking system offers a model for responding to deadly inundations.

item.title: I Played Putin in a War Game. He Could Invade Again.
item.description: NATO countries need to get their act together.

item.title: The Justices Acted as Partisans in the Voting Rights Ruling
item.description: In the name of disentangling race from politics, the court has given white voters more power at the expense of racial minorities.

item.title: What Worries Me Most About ‘Abundance’
item.description: It’s been a big year for the abundance movement, but what has it really achieved? Ezra Klein talks with his “Abundance” co-author Derek Thompson and with Marc Dunkelman, the author of “Why Nothing Works.”

item.title: The Fascinating Stories of Dying Tongues
item.description: On finding new horizons and saving a disappearing language.

item.title: Older Americans Are a Reality, Not a Problem
item.description: Sampling the outpouring of responses to a guest essay, “Older Americans Are Hoarding the Nation’s Potential,” by Samuel Moyn. Also: A clash over a wind project.

item.title: I’m Not a King, but I Play One on TV
item.description: Is there something to this whole monarchy thing?

item.title: Silicon Valley Is Bracing for a Permanent Underclass
item.description: The people building A.I. fear that we have only a short time before advanced A.I. disrupts the labor force.

item.title: Why Are We Still Driving?
item.description: Confronting the weirdness of a Waymo future.

item.title: Why Are We Still Driving?
item.description: Confronting the weirdness of a Waymo future.

item.title: Two Cocky Authoritarians Blocking Hormuz: What Could Go Wrong?
item.description: In the Iran war, we have two overconfident administrations facing off, each believing that time is on its side.

item.title: Why Texas Is Winning the Housing War
item.description: Rents are falling in Texas — and the reason is simpler than you think. Derek Thompson, a contributing writer at The Atlantic, tells the Opinion columnist Ezra Klein how Dallas and Austin have shown that the only real cure for a housing crisis is an aggressive, unrelenting surge in supply.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Should Die. Why Is Trump Trying to Save It?
item.description: Trump’s effort to bail out the ailing carrier makes no sense.

item.title: The Many Ways the Iran War Is Changing the World
item.description: It won’t be so easy to extract ourselves from this conflict.

item.title: Comey and Kimmel: Seashells and a Joke
item.description: Readers discuss the James Comey indictment and the Jimmy Kimmel joke. Also: The U.S.A.I.D. tragedy; privatizing the mail.

item.title: A Journalist Covered a Missile Strike. Then the Death Threats Started.
item.description: Washington should govern prediction markets as if national security were at stake, because it is.

item.title: The Supreme Court Should Stop ICE From Racially Profiling
item.description: Body camera footage reveals a disturbing pattern of state and federal officials using minor traffic stops to target Black and brown drivers.

item.title: What Body Cam Footage Reveals About ICE’s Tactics
item.description: New body camera footage from Nashville reveals a disturbing pattern of ICE and state troopers using minor traffic stops to target Black and brown drivers.

item.title: Political Campaigns Have No Idea What’s About to Hit Them
item.description: Social media helped put Trump over the top. What’s A.I. going to do to us?

item.title: The Banality of Evil, Again
item.description: The distinguishing feature of Cole Tomas Allen’s manifesto is its insipidity.

item.title: What We Got Right — and Wrong — in ‘Abundance’
item.description: It’s been a big year for the abundance movement, but what has it really achieved? Ezra Klein talks with his “Abundance” co-author Derek Thompson and with Marc Dunkelman, the author of “Why Nothing Works.”

item.title: It’s Been Centuries Since Haiti’s Revolution. It’s Still Paying for It.
item.description: Haiti’s revolution shocked the world. America still isn’t over it.

item.title: Children at Risk as Measles Cases Rise
item.description: “I would not wish measles upon my fiercest enemy,” a reader writes of the recent resurgence.

item.title: After Mythos, Nobody Is Safe From Cybersecurity Threats
item.description: Nobody can afford to be relaxed about their digital security anymore.

item.title: After an Assassination Attempt, a Grim Political Math Sets In
item.description: Assassination attempts against sitting presidents have tended to compound their political problems and isolate them from the public.

item.title: What a Catholic Church Unafraid of Donald Trump Means to the World
item.description: The resonance of the pontiff-versus-president imbroglio reveals a hunger for moral authority at a time that feels deeply disoriented and spiritually thin.

item.title: What Tucker Carlson Means When He Talks About Israel
item.description: The far right is conflating Israel with Jewishness.

item.title: Political Violence Is Reprehensible. That Doesn’t Make Trump Less Depraved.
item.description: Each act of political violence further frays our threadbare social fabric, laying the foundation for authoritarianism.

item.title: Can King Charles III Still Hold Sway With Trump?
item.description: Courtesy can be tactical as well as virtuous.

item.title: Maybe Trump Was Never a Deal Maker
item.description: Trump built his political brand on being the ultimate negotiator, but in his second term that image is crumbling. On “The Opinions,” the columnist Carlos Lozada argues that we’re witnessing “the limits to his deal-making prowess, especially internationally.”

item.title: The Frightening Night at a Washington Gala
item.description: Readers discuss political violence, online rumors, gun control and security measures.
